title: Beef and Guinness Stew
subtitle: Slow-braised beef in a deep, bittersweet Guinness and stock gravy
excerpt: Beef and Guinness stew uses the stout's bitterness and body to give the gravy a depth that stock alone cannot provide. The Guinness reduces to a dark, complex sauce over 2 hours. It is a richer, more assertive stew than a plain beef braise.
sourceNotes: Irish and British tradition. The use of Guinness or dark stout in a beef braise is documented in Irish and British cookbooks from the mid-20th century, though beef stewed in dark ale has older roots in British regional cooking.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well. The flavour intensifies after freezing and reheating.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Makes the day before. Refrigerate and reheat gently; the stew improves overnight.

glossaryTerms.definition: A slow-cooking method where food is browned first, then cooked in liquid in a covered vessel over low heat. The moist heat gradually breaks down collagen in tougher cuts of meat, producing tender meat and a concentrated, flavoursome liquid.

content.text: Beef and Guinness stew is the most flavoursome of the beef braises in this section. The stout's bitterness, roasted malt notes, and carbonation all contribute to the gravy during the long braise. The bitterness softens considerably over 2 hours but never disappears entirely; it is the thing that distinguishes this from a plain beef stew. Use a full can of Guinness.

Heat the oven to 160°C. Season the {{braising-steak}} of beef and toss in the {{plain-flour}} of flour. Heat a film of oil in a wide frying pan over a high heat and brown the beef in batches for 3 to 4 minutes per batch, turning once. Transfer to a casserole.

In the same pan, fry the {{onion}} onion and {{carrot}} carrot for 5 minutes until softened and lightly coloured. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and tomato puree and stir for 1 minute. Pour in the {{guinness}} of Guinness and bring to a brief boil, scraping up the pan residue.
Pour the liquid and vegetables over the beef. Add the {{stock-beef}} of beef stock, Worcestershire sauce, thyme, and bay. Season. The liquid should cover about two-thirds of the meat. Cover and
in the oven at 160°C for 2 hours until the beef is completely tender and the gravy is dark and reduced.
Remove the lid for the final 20 minutes to concentrate the gravy further. Discard the thyme stalks and bay leaves. Taste and season. Rest for 10 minutes before serving.

items.cause: Guinness was not reduced enough before the lid went on, or the quality of the stout is very bitter.
items.fix: Let the Guinness boil for 2 full minutes before adding the stock and transferring to the casserole. A small amount of tomato puree also helps balance the bitterness.

items.cause: Too much liquid or lid left on throughout.
items.fix: Remove the lid for the final 20 to 30 minutes. If still too thin, lift the beef out, reduce the gravy on the hob, and return the beef to the pot.

items.symptom: Beef is still tough after 2 hours
items.cause: Heat too low, or the cuts were too large.
items.fix: The pieces should be no larger than 4 cm. If still tough, return to the oven for another 30 minutes. Braising steak releases its connective tissue fully only at sustained low heat.

content.text: Beef braised in dark ale has a long history in British regional cooking; the Guinness version became identified with Irish cooking in Britain from the mid-20th century as Irish stout became widely available. The association makes sense: Guinness has a roasted, bitter character that stands up to the iron richness of braising beef where lighter ales do not. The stew is deeply savoury and slightly bitter in a way that plain beef gravy is not. It is a winter dish, substantial and slow, best served with mashed potato or crusty bread.
